title: SQL Joins — INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL
description: All SQL join types: combining tables, understanding join behavior, and multi-table queries.

questions.text: Which type of JOIN returns only the rows that have matching values in BOTH tables?

options.text: LEFT JOIN
RIGHT JOIN
INNER JOIN
FULL OUTER JOIN

questions.text: Which JOIN returns ALL rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table (NULLs for non-matches on the right)?

options.text: INNER JOIN
LEFT JOIN
RIGHT JOIN
CROSS JOIN

questions.text: Which JOIN returns ALL rows from the right table and only matching rows from the left table?

options.text: LEFT JOIN
FULL OUTER JOIN
RIGHT JOIN
INNER JOIN

questions.text: Which JOIN returns ALL rows from BOTH tables, with NULLs where there is no match?

options.text: INNER JOIN
CROSS JOIN
LEFT JOIN
FULL OUTER JOIN

questions.text: What does a CROSS JOIN produce?

options.text: Only rows where there is a match between tables
The Cartesian product — every row from the left table combined with every row from the right table
All rows from the left table with NULLs for missing right rows
A set difference between two tables

questions.text: How many rows does a CROSS JOIN of a 5-row table and a 4-row table produce?
What is a self join?

options.text: A join that automatically selects the correct join type
A join where a table is joined to itself using aliases
A join with no ON condition
A join that only returns one row

questions.text: Which query correctly finds each employee and their manager using a self join on the Employees table (ManagerID references EmployeeID)?

options.text: SELECT e.Name, m.Name AS Manager FROM Employees e INNER JOIN Employees m ON e.ManagerID = m.EmployeeID
SELECT e.Name, m.Name AS Manager FROM Employees e, Employees m WHERE e.EmployeeID = m.ManagerID
SELECT Name, Manager FROM Employees SELF JOIN
SELECT * FROM Employees JOIN Employees ON ManagerID

questions.text: In a LEFT JOIN, if no matching row exists in the right table, what appears in the right-table columns?

options.text: Empty string ''
NULL
The row is excluded from results

questions.text: Which query finds customers who have NO orders (using a LEFT JOIN)?

options.text: SELECT c.* FROM Customers c INNER JOIN Orders o ON c.ID = o.CustomerID
SELECT c.* FROM Customers c LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.ID = o.CustomerID WHERE o.ID IS NULL
SELECT c.* FROM Customers c RIGHT JOIN Orders o ON c.ID = o.CustomerID WHERE o.ID IS NULL
SELECT c.* FROM Customers c FULL JOIN Orders o ON c.ID = o.CustomerID WHERE c.ID IS NOT NULL

questions.text: What does ON specify in a JOIN clause?

options.text: The columns to display in the result
The condition used to match rows between the two tables
The order in which tables are scanned
The index to use for the join

questions.text: Which keyword can be used instead of ON when the join column has the same name in both tables?

options.text: MATCH
NATURAL
USING
EQUAL

questions.text: How do you join three tables — Orders, Customers, and Products — in a single query?

options.text: You cannot join more than two tables in SQL
SELECT ... FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON ... JOIN Products ON ...
SELECT ... FROM Orders, Customers, Products WHERE ...
Both b and c are valid approaches

questions.text: What is the key difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?

options.text: INNER JOIN is faster but LEFT JOIN is more accurate
INNER JOIN only returns matched rows; LEFT JOIN returns all left-table rows including unmatched ones
LEFT JOIN requires both tables to have identical schemas
There is no difference; they return the same results

questions.text: Which join condition joins two tables on multiple columns? Example: ON a.ID = b.ID AND a.Year = b.Year

options.text: This syntax is invalid; joins can only use one column
This is a composite join condition and is perfectly valid
MULTI JOIN ON (ID, Year)
JOIN USING (ID, Year) is the only valid way

questions.text: What does a table alias do in a JOIN query? Example: FROM Employees e JOIN Departments d ON e.DeptID = d.ID

options.text: Creates a permanent view of the table
Provides a shorthand name for the table to make the query shorter and more readable
Renames the table in the database permanently
Creates a copy of the table

questions.text: In which scenario would you choose a subquery OVER a JOIN?

options.text: When you need to return columns from both tables
When you only need to check existence or aggregate from a related table without displaying its columns
Subqueries are always better than joins
When joining more than two tables

questions.text: Which JOIN type is equivalent to INNER JOIN when used with no filter?

options.text: FULL OUTER JOIN
LEFT JOIN
A comma-separated FROM clause with a matching WHERE condition
CROSS JOIN

questions.text: What is the result of a FULL OUTER JOIN between Customers (3 rows) and Orders (5 rows) where 2 customers have orders?

options.text: 2 rows
5 rows
All rows from both tables — customers without orders get NULLs, orders without customers get NULLs
15 rows (Cartesian product)

questions.text: What happens if you JOIN two tables without specifying an ON condition (in databases that require it)?

options.text: It defaults to an INNER JOIN on the primary key
It produces a syntax error
It produces a Cartesian product (same as CROSS JOIN)
It returns no rows

questions.text: Which query uses aliases correctly to disambiguate columns with the same name from two tables?

options.text: SELECT Name FROM Employees JOIN Departments ON Employees.DeptID = Departments.ID
SELECT e.Name AS EmpName, d.Name AS DeptName FROM Employees e JOIN Departments d ON e.DeptID = d.ID
SELECT Name AS (Employees, Departments) FROM Employees JOIN Departments ON ID
SELECT Name1, Name2 FROM Employees JOIN Departments

questions.text: LEFT JOIN and LEFT OUTER JOIN — are they different?

options.text: Yes, LEFT JOIN is exclusive while LEFT OUTER JOIN includes NULLs
No, they are identical; OUTER is optional
Yes, LEFT OUTER JOIN returns more rows
LEFT OUTER JOIN is deprecated

questions.text: Which join type would you use to find all rows that exist in either table, regardless of whether they have a match?

options.text: INNER JOIN
LEFT JOIN
FULL OUTER JOIN
CROSS JOIN

questions.text: Is it valid to include additional filter conditions in the ON clause of a LEFT JOIN?

options.text: No, extra conditions must go in the WHERE clause
Yes, and the behavior differs from putting the condition in WHERE — ON filters before joining while WHERE filters after
Yes, both ON and WHERE are equivalent in LEFT JOIN
Extra conditions in ON cause a syntax error

questions.text: What does: SELECT * FROM A LEFT JOIN B ON A.ID = B.ID WHERE B.ID IS NULL — return?

options.text: All rows from A that have a matching row in B
All rows from B that have no match in A
All rows from A that have NO matching row in B
All rows from both A and B

questions.text: Which statement is TRUE about INNER JOIN performance compared to LEFT JOIN?

options.text: INNER JOIN is always slower than LEFT JOIN
INNER JOIN may perform better since it eliminates non-matching rows earlier, but the optimizer often handles both similarly
LEFT JOIN is always faster because it accesses fewer rows
They always have identical performance

questions.text: Can you JOIN a table to a subquery (derived table)?

options.text: No, JOINs can only be used with physical tables
Yes — you can join to a derived table: FROM TableA JOIN (SELECT ...) AS sub ON ...
Yes, but only with INNER JOIN
Only in MySQL, not in SQL Server

questions.text: Tables: Products (ProductID, Name, CategoryID) and Categories (CategoryID, CategoryName). Which query gets product names with category names?

options.text: SELECT p.Name, c.CategoryName FROM Products p JOIN Categories c ON p.ProductID = c.CategoryID
SELECT p.Name, c.CategoryName FROM Products p JOIN Categories c ON p.CategoryID = c.CategoryID
SELECT Name, CategoryName FROM Products, Categories
SELECT p.Name, c.CategoryName FROM Products p CROSS JOIN Categories c

questions.text: What is a NATURAL JOIN?

options.text: A join that automatically matches all columns with the same name in both tables
A join without any WHERE clause
A join on primary key and foreign key automatically
A join that returns NULL for all non-matching columns

questions.text: When joining multiple tables, what is the recommended practice to avoid ambiguous column references?

options.text: Always use SELECT * to avoid specifying columns
Prefix column names with the table name or alias (e.g., e.Name, d.Name)
Rename all columns in the original tables to be unique
Ambiguous columns are automatically resolved by SQL
